verses.flow: The word of YHWH that came to Hosea (Hoshea), son of Beeri, in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, and in the days of Jeroboam son of Joash, king of Israel.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Hosea's ministry spans approximately 760–720 BC, overlapping with Isaiah and Amos. The listing of four Judahite kings before the Israelite king Jeroboam II reflects either a Judean editorial hand or deliberate theological priority, situating Hosea's message within the broader covenantal framework of the Davidic monarchy. The Northern Kingdom of Israel would fall to Assyria in 722 BC, within the prophetic horizon of this book.

verses.flow: When YHWH first spoke through Hosea, YHWH said to Hosea, "Go, take for yourself a wife of whoredom and children of whoredom, for the land commits great whoredom by forsaking YHWH."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to marry a woman of whoredom (Heb. 'eshet zenunim) is one of the most debated in all prophetic literature — whether literal or visionary. Either way, Hosea's marriage becomes a living parable (mashal) of YHWH's covenant relationship with Israel. The verb "forsaking" (zanah, to commit harlotry) is the standard covenantal infidelity term, equating Baal worship with spiritual adultery against the divine husband (ba'al in its older sense of "lord/husband"). (Jer 3:1), (Eph 5:25–32)

verses.flow: And YHWH said to him, "Call his name Jezreel (Yizre'el), for yet a little while and I will visit the blood of Jezreel upon the house of Jehu, and I will put an end to the kingdom of the house of Israel."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Jezreel" (Yizre'el) means "God sows" — a place of violent royal history (2 Kgs 9–10). YHWH promises to "visit" (paqad) the bloodshed of Jehu's coup upon his dynasty. Paqad carries judicial weight: divine reckoning and accountability. Jehu's purge, though commanded, was executed with excessive ambition, and divine justice is not voided by divine commission. THEOLOGICAL: The name Jezreel carries triple meaning across the chapter — judgment (v. 4), military defeat (v. 5), and eschatological hope (v. 11).

verses.flow: She conceived again and bore a daughter. And YHWH said to him, "Call her name Lo-Ruhamah (Lo-Ruchamah), for I will no more have mercy (racham) on the house of Israel, to forgive them at all."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lo-Ruhamah" means "No Mercy" or "Not Pitied" — the negation of racham (Strong's H7355), the deep womb-love cognate with rechem (womb). This is among the most tender Hebrew words for compassion, making its negation devastating. The withdrawal of divine racham signals covenantal rupture at the most intimate level. TYPOLOGY: Paul cites this verse in Romans 9:25–26 alongside Hosea 2:23 to argue that Gentiles are now included in the mercy once withdrawn from unfaithful Israel, demonstrating the eschatological reversal of covenant exclusion.

verses.flow: And YHWH said, "Call his name Lo-Ammi (Lo-'Ammi), for you are not my people ('am), and I am not your I AM."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Lo-Ammi" (Not My People) directly inverts the covenant formula of Exodus 6:7 and Leviticus 26:12 — "I will be your God and you shall be my people." The final clause in Hebrew is shockingly terse: 'anoki lo' ehyeh lakem — "I am not I-AM to you," a reversal of the divine self-disclosure of Exodus 3:14 ('ehyeh 'asher 'ehyeh). YHWH effectively withdraws the covenant name itself. This is the theological nadir of the chapter. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The severing of divine patronage echoes the Deuteronomy 32:8–9 framework — nations abandoned to lesser elohim; Israel uniquely YHWH's portion — now that unique status is threatened.

verses.flow: Say to your brothers, "My people," and to your sisters, "She has obtained compassion (ruhamah)."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruhamah" (Strong's H7355, from raham, "womb-love, compassion") directly reverses the name of Hosea's daughter Lo-Ruhamah (1:6, "No Compassion"). TYPOLOGY: Paul cites this verse together with v.23 in Romans 9:25–26 as fulfilled in the inclusion of the Gentiles, who were "not a people" but are now called "sons of the living God."

verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "contend" (ribu, Strong's H7378) is legal terminology, evoking a covenant lawsuit (rib), a formal indictment in the divine court. verses.flow: For their mother has played the harlot; she who conceived them has acted shamefully. For she said, "I will go after my lovers, who give me my bread and my water, my wool and my flax, my oil and my drink."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The lovers (Baals) were the Canaanite fertility deities whom Israel credited with agricultural abundance — grain, wine, flax, oil. The Baal cult promised material prosperity through sexual ritual. Israel's theological error was misattributing YHWH's covenant blessings to pagan gods. THEOLOGICAL: This verse illustrates the root of all idolatry: crediting creation's gifts to a source other than the Covenant Creator.

verses.flow: And she did not know that it was I who gave her the grain and the new wine and the fresh oil, and who lavished upon her silver and gold, which they used for Baal.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tragic irony of verse 8 is the theological center of the oracle — Israel used YHWH's own gifts to fund the worship of Baal. "Silver and gold" recalls the golden calves of Jeroboam. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The name Baal (ba'al, "lord, master, owner") was the title of the Canaanite storm god El's son Hadad, a member of the divine council who had been assigned authority over nations but was worshiped in defiance of YHWH's exclusive covenant claim on Israel.

verses.flow: Therefore, behold, I will allure her and bring her into the wilderness, and speak tenderly (al-libah) to her heart.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Speak tenderly" is literally "speak to her heart" (dibbur al-lev), an idiom of intimate, wooing speech used of courtship (Gen 34:3; Ruth 2:13). The wilderness is not punishment here but the place of renewed intimacy — an echo of the Sinai covenant. TYPOLOGY: The wilderness allurement mirrors the Exodus and anticipates the eschatological new exodus, in which Jesus the Messiah leads his people through death into renewed covenant life.

verses.flow: And I will give her her vineyards from there, and the Valley of Achor as a door of hope. And there she shall respond as in the days of her youth, as at the time she came up out of the land of Egypt.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Valley of Achor ("trouble," from the account of Achan in Josh 7) was the site of judgment at Israel's entry into the Promised Land. Its transformation into "a door of hope" signals that the place of curse becomes the threshold of blessing. TYPOLOGY: This inversion of judgment into hope is paradigmatic for the NT's theology of the cross — the site of condemnation becomes the door of resurrection life (cf. 1 Cor 1:18).

verses.flow: And it shall come to pass in that day — a declaration of YHWH — that you shall call me "My Husband (Ishi)," and shall no longer call me "My Master (Baali)."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ishi" (ishi, "my man/husband") and "Baali" (baali, "my lord/master/Baal") are distinguished here. YHWH rejects the title Baali because it had become indistinguishable from the name of the pagan deity Baal. The shift from Baali to Ishi represents a move from ownership/dominion language to intimate personal relationship — a profound covenantal shift. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The purging of the name Baal from Israel's lips (v.17) represents YHWH's decisive demotion of the Baal figures from any role in Israel's covenantal life.

verses.flow: And I will make for them a covenant in that day with the beasts of the field, the birds of the heavens, and the creeping things of the ground. And I will abolish the bow, the sword, and warfare from the land, and I will make them lie down in safety (betach).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The covenant with beasts and the abolition of warfare echoes the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:9–10) and anticipates the messianic peace of Isaiah 11:6–9 and Ezekiel 34:25. This is a new creation promise. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The abolition of the "bow, sword, and warfare" may imply the defeat of the spiritual powers behind warfare (cf. Eph 6:12), not merely cessation of human conflict.

verses.flow: And I will betroth you to me forever. I will betroth you to me in righteousness (tsedek) and in justice (mishpat), in steadfast love (hesed) and in compassion (rahamim).
verses.note: HEBREW: Five covenant virtues are named in betrothal: tsedek (righteousness), mishpat (justice), hesed (covenant loyalty/steadfast love), rahamim (compassion/womb-love), and emunah (v.20, faithfulness/trustworthiness). These are not merely ethical qualities but covenantal attributes of YHWH himself, given as the bride-price of the renewed marriage. THEOLOGICAL: The repetition of "I will betroth you" three times (vv.19–20) in ascending intimacy is widely regarded as one of the most profound expressions of divine love in the Hebrew scriptures.

verses.flow: I will betroth you to me in faithfulness (emunah), and you shall know YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "You shall know YHWH" (yedaat YHWH) is the summative goal of the entire betrothal — covenant knowledge that is intimate, experiential, and transformative. This is the same phrase Hosea uses in 6:6 and Jeremiah echoes in the new covenant promise (Jer 31:34). TYPOLOGY: The "knowledge of YHWH" finds its New Covenant fulfillment in Jesus Christ, in whom all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell (Col 1:19), and through whom the nations come to know the Father (John 17:3).

verses.flow: And I will sow her for myself in the land, and I will have compassion on "No Compassion (Lo-Ruhamah)," and I will say to "Not My People (Lo-Ammi)," "You are my people"; and he shall say, "You are my God."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The reversal of all three names of shame — Jezreel to "I will sow," Lo-Ruhamah to "She has obtained compassion," and Lo-Ammi to "You are my people" — constitutes the climactic eschatological reversal of the entire Hosea cycle. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:25 and Peter alludes to it in 1 Peter 2:10, both applying it to Gentiles incorporated into the covenant people of God through faith in the Messiah Jesus. THEOLOGICAL: The final confession "You are my God" (Elohay attah) mirrors the covenant formula and anticipates Revelation 21:3 — the ultimate fulfillment of YHWH's dwelling with his people.

verses.flow: And YHWH said to me, "Go again, love a woman who is loved by a companion and is committing adultery, even as YHWH loves the sons of Israel, though they turn to other gods and love raisin-cakes."
verses.note: HEBREW: The command "Go again" (לֵךְ עוֹד, lech 'od) resumes the enacted prophecy from chapter 1, structuring Hosea's marriage as an ongoing sign-act. The term "raisin-cakes" (אֲשִׁישֵׁי עֲנָבִים) likely refers to cultic offerings used in fertility rites, connecting Israel's apostasy to Canaanite worship practices (cf. Song 2:5; Jer 44:19). The phrase "loved by a companion" may indicate the woman is already in an adulterous relationship, intensifying the grace of Hosea's redemptive act as a mirror of YHWH's love. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's love (אַהֲבָה, ahavah) for Israel is presented here as uncaused and persistent, extended despite Israel's spiritual adultery with foreign deities — a foundational statement of covenantal grace (hesed) preceding any repentance.

verses.flow: So I bought her for myself for fifteen pieces of silver and a homer of barley and a lethech of barley.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The price of fifteen shekels of silver plus barley is notable; thirty shekels was the standard valuation of a slave (Exod 21:32). The reduced price may suggest the woman's degraded condition after harlotry, or that Hosea paid in a combination of goods reflecting economic hardship. A homer was approximately 220 liters of grain; a lethech (לֶתֶךְ) is a half-homer, attested only here in the Hebrew Bible (hapax legomenon in this precise commercial sense), underscoring the unique nature of this transaction. TYPOLOGY: The act of purchasing or redeeming a degraded beloved at personal cost anticipates the redemptive work of Christ, who bought His bride the Church "not with silver or gold, but with His precious blood" (1 Pet 1:18–19).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: And I said to her, "Many days you shall dwell as mine; you shall not play the whore, and you shall not belong to a man, and so also I will be toward you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Hosea's declaration "I will be toward you" (וְגַם אֲנִי אֵלַיִךְ) employs deliberate incompleteness — the verb is withheld — mirroring a posture of patient, covenantal waiting rather than full restoration. This period of enforced fidelity without full conjugal relationship models Israel's coming exile: preserved but not yet fully restored. The condition of disciplined separation is an act of love aimed at ultimate reunion, not permanent abandonment.

verses.flow: For many days the sons of Israel shall dwell without king and without prince, and without sacrifice and without pillar, and without ephod and household idols (teraphim).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The four-fold deprivation — king, prince, sacrifice, pillar — encompasses both legitimate Israelite institutions and illegitimate ones (the standing-stone or massebah was condemned, yet listed alongside the ephod), suggesting a total stripping of all religious and political structures during exile. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "teraphim" (תְּרָפִים, teraphim) were household gods associated with ancestral veneration and divination, attested widely in the ANE and referenced in Gen 31:19 and 1 Sam 19:13. Their inclusion here acknowledges that Israel had syncretized YHWH worship with the veneration of lesser spiritual beings, a violation of the divine council order in which YHWH alone is sovereign.

verses.flow: Afterward the sons of Israel shall return and seek YHWH their God, and David their king, and they shall come in fear (pachad) to YHWH and to His goodness in the latter days.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The reference to "David their king" is a messianic title pointing beyond the historical dynasty to the coming Davidic Messiah, as David had been dead for centuries at Hosea's writing. This anticipates the eschatological Davidic king (cf. Jer 30:9; Ezek 34:23–24; 37:24) fulfilled in Jesus Christ, who is "the Root and the Offspring of David" (Rev 22:16). HEBREW: The phrase "in the latter days" (בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים, be'acharit hayyamim) is a terminus technicus for the eschatological age, appearing also in Gen 49:1, Num 24:14, and Deut 4:30, consistently marking a horizon of ultimate fulfillment beyond the immediate prophetic context. The word pachad (פַּחַד, fear/trembling) connotes reverent awe before the overwhelming goodness of YHWH, not mere terror.

verses.flow: Hear the word of YHWH, O children of Israel, for YHWH has a controversy (rib) with the inhabitants of the land; for there is no faithfulness (emet), and no steadfast love (hesed), and no knowledge of God in the land.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "rib" (Strong's H7379) is a legal covenant-lawsuit term drawn from the ancient Near Eastern treaty tradition. YHWH here functions as a prosecuting party bringing formal charges against Israel for covenant violation. The three absent qualities — emet (faithfulness/truth, H571), hesed (covenant loyalty/steadfast love, H2617), and da'at elohim (knowledge of God, H1847) — constitute the core relational obligations of the Sinai covenant. THEOLOGICAL: This triad (emet, hesed, da'at) recurs throughout Hosea as the defining virtues of covenant faithfulness (cf. Hos 6:6; Mic 6:8). Their absence signals comprehensive covenant breakdown, not merely moral failure but relational apostasy.

verses.flow: Swearing and lying, and killing, and stealing, and committing adultery — they break out, and blood touches blood.
verses.note: HEBREW: The five sins listed correspond closely to violations of the Decalogue (Exod 20:1–17; Deut 5:6–21): false oaths, lying, murder, theft, and adultery. The phrase "blood touches blood" (dam b'dam naga, H1818) is a stark idiom indicating that acts of bloodshed are so incessant they overlap without interruption. HISTORICAL: Such covenant-lawsuit language (cf. Isa 1:2–20; Mic 6:1–8) echoes the suzerainty treaty form of the ANE, wherein a sovereign king issues formal charges against a vassal who has broken treaty obligations.

verses.flow: Therefore the land mourns, and all who dwell in it languish, together with the beasts of the field and the birds of the heavens; and even the fish of the sea are taken away.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The mourning of the land and the perishing of animals, birds, and fish echoes the creation order of Genesis 1 in reverse — a de-creation motif in which covenant unfaithfulness produces ecological unraveling. TYPOLOGY: This anticipates the eschatological groaning of creation under sin described in Romans 8:19–22, linking covenantal faithfulness to the well-being of the created order.

verses.flow: My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge; because you have rejected knowledge, I also will reject you from serving as priest to me; and since you have forgotten the law (torah) of your God, I also will forget your children.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Destroyed" translates the Niphal of damah (H1820), meaning to be cut off, silenced, or ruined. "Knowledge" here is da'at (H1847), rooted in the verb yada' (to know intimately), implying not merely intellectual information but experiential, relational, covenantal knowing of God. "Torah" (H8451) signifies instruction and divine teaching, not merely law as legal code. THEOLOGICAL: The rejection of knowledge and forgetting of Torah are parallel failures: priestly apostasy breeds communal ignorance. God's reciprocal rejection of the priest mirrors the lex talionis principle — the punishment mirrors the sin. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the Great High Priest perfectly embodies and transmits the knowledge of God that Israel's priesthood failed to preserve (John 1:18; Heb 4:14–16).

verses.flow: And it shall be: like people, like priest; I will punish them for their ways and repay them for their deeds.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Like people, like priest" is a proverbial indictment inverting the proper order: priests were to elevate the people toward God, but Israel's priests had descended to the moral level of the people. HISTORICAL: The Aaronic priesthood bore the responsibility of teaching Torah and discerning clean from unclean (Lev 10:10–11; Mal 2:7). Their failure cascaded into national apostasy, a pattern seen also in the house of Eli (1 Sam 2–3).

verses.flow: They shall eat, but not be satisfied; they shall play the whore, but not multiply; because they have forsaken YHWH to guard (shamar).
Whoredom and wine and new wine take away the heart (leb).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Heart" translates leb (H3820), the Hebrew seat of intellect, will, and understanding — not merely emotion as in modern English usage. The verse is terse and aphoristic: whoredom (zenut), wine (yayin), and new wine (tirosh) together rob Israel of its moral and cognitive faculties. THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of sexual immorality and intoxication as instruments of spiritual stupefaction is consistent with the fertility cult worship of Baal, which employed both in ritual contexts.

verses.flow: My people inquire of a piece of wood, and their staff gives them oracles. For a spirit (ruach) of whoredom has led them astray, and they have played the whore, departing from under their God.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "spirit (ruach) of whoredom" (ruach zenunim, H7307 + H2183) is likely more than a metaphor — it denotes an actual spiritual power or demonic influence driving Israel into idolatry. This language is consistent with Second Temple Jewish understandings of territorial spirits and the divine council framework (cf. Deut 32:17; Ps 106:37; 1 Cor 10:20). HISTORICAL: Divination by wooden objects (rhabdomancy) and staff-oracles were known practices among Israel's neighbors and are condemned throughout the prophetic corpus (cf. Ezek 21:21).

verses.flow: On the tops of the mountains they sacrifice, and on the hills they make offerings, under oak, and poplar, and terebinth, because their shade is good. Therefore your daughters play the whore, and your brides commit adultery.

I will not punish your daughters when they play the whore, nor your brides when they commit adultery; for the men themselves go aside with prostitutes and sacrifice with cult prostitutes, and a people without understanding (bin) shall come to ruin.
Though you play the whore, O Israel, let not Judah become guilty. Enter not into Gilgal, nor go up to Beth-aven, and swear not, "As YHWH lives."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gilgal was a sacred site in the Jordan Valley associated with Israel's entry into the land (Josh 4–5) but had become a center of apostate worship by the 8th century (cf. Amos 4:4; 5:5). "Beth-aven" (House of Iniquity/Wickedness) is a derisive renaming of Bethel (House of God), where Jeroboam I had erected the golden calf (1 Kgs 12:28–29). THEOLOGICAL: The warning to Judah not to follow Israel's example establishes a distinction that runs throughout the Twelve: Judah retains a remnant hope tied to the Davidic covenant, even as the northern kingdom faces imminent judgment.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For like a stubborn heifer, Israel has become stubborn; now YHWH will pasture them like a lamb in a broad place?
Ephraim is joined to idols; leave him alone.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Ephraim being "joined" (chaber, H2266) to idols may carry the nuance of being spiritually bonded or yoked to demonic powers behind the idols (cf. Ps 106:28; 1 Cor 10:19–20). The command "leave him alone" is YHWH's judicial abandonment — a withdrawal of divine protection and pleading, resulting in Ephraim's exposure to the full consequences of his spiritual alliances. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most chilling declarations in the prophetic corpus, echoing the threefold "God gave them over" of Romans 1:24–28 as the ultimate form of divine judgment.

verses.flow: When their drinking has ended, they engage in wanton prostitution; their shields (rulers) dearly love shame.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: A wind (ruach) has wrapped them in its wings, and they shall be ashamed because of their altars.
verses.note: HEBREW: The closing verse employs ruach (H7307) again, now as destructive wind rather than indwelling spirit — forming a deliberate inclusio with verse 12. "Wrapped in its wings" is a violent image of being swept away and enveloped. THEOLOGICAL: The shame of the altars — the very sites of their false worship — will be their undoing. Judgment comes through the very instrument of their idolatry. TYPOLOGY: The motif of shame as covenantal consequence anticipates the eschatological reversal where the faithful are given garments of praise in place of shame (Isa 61:3; Rev 7:9–14).

words.g: and to prick up the ears
words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Hear this, O priests, and give heed, O house of Israel, and O house of the king, give ear — for the judgment (mishpat) is upon you, for you have been a snare at Mizpah and a net spread upon Tabor.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The three addressees — priests, house of Israel, house of the king — form a legal tribunal audience, reflecting the covenant lawsuit (rib) form common in the eighth-century prophets. Mizpah and Tabor were likely sites of illicit cultic activity, possibly involving the worship of Baal, making them centers of spiritual entrapment for pilgrims. THEOLOGICAL: The term mishpat (H4941) here carries the sense of a formal legal verdict, not merely justice in the abstract — YHWH is acting as the sovereign judge of his covenant people.

verses.flow: And the revolters have gone deep into slaughter, though I am a chastisement (musar) to all of them.
I myself know Ephraim, and Israel is not hidden from me; for now, O Ephraim, you have played the harlot, Israel is defiled.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "to know" (yada, H3045) in Hebrew carries covenantal intimacy, not mere cognitive awareness — YHWH's knowing of Ephraim implies a relational claim that renders their sin without excuse. The charge of "played the harlot" (zanah, H2181) is the same root used throughout Hosea 1-3 of Gomer, binding the nation's spiritual apostasy directly to the marriage metaphor. THEOLOGICAL: The declaration "Israel is defiled" (tame, H2930) is a cultic term of ritual impurity, suggesting that their sin has rendered them unfit for approach to YHWH's presence.

verses.flow: Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God, for a spirit (ruach) of harlotry is in their midst, and YHWH they do not know.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "spirit (ruach) of harlotry" is not merely a metaphor for moral tendency but reflects the biblical worldview in which spiritual forces can drive nations toward false worship — comparable to the "lying spirit" of 1 Kings 22:22 and the territorial spirits of Deuteronomy 32:17. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "YHWH they do not know" inverts the covenantal intimacy of verse 3; though YHWH knows them, they have forfeited reciprocal knowledge through idolatry, echoing the theme of Hosea 4:1.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And the pride (gaon) of Israel testifies to his face; and Israel and Ephraim shall stumble in their iniquity, Judah also shall stumble with them.

words.g: in/with a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: With their flocks and with their herds they shall go to seek YHWH, but they shall not find him — he has withdrawn himself from them.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Against YHWH they have dealt treacherously, for they have borne strange children; now the new moon shall devour them together with their portions.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Strange children" (banim zarim, H2114) likely refers to children born from cultic prostitution or, more broadly, to a generation formed in the image of Baal worship rather than covenant fidelity. TYPOLOGY: The "new moon" as an instrument of judgment is ironic — the new moon festival was a designated sacred assembly (Numbers 28:11), now turned against those who perverted its worship. The devouring of "their portions" may refer to the allotted tribal lands, which YHWH originally gave as inheritance.

verses.flow: Blow the horn (shofar) in Gibeah, the trumpet (chatzotzrah) in Ramah; sound the alarm at Beth-aven — behind you, O Benjamin!
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gibeah, Ramah, and Beth-aven form a north-to-south corridor in Benjamin's territory, suggesting the warning of military invasion from the north sweeping southward. Beth-aven ("house of wickedness/nothingness") is Hosea's contemptuous renaming of Bethel ("house of God"), the site of Jeroboam's golden calves (1 Kings 12:28-29). STRUCTURE: The alarm call signals the Day of YHWH motif — the shofar (H7782) throughout the prophets announces both military emergency and divine judgment.

verses.flow: Ephraim shall become a desolation in the day of rebuke; among the tribes of Israel I make known what is sure.
The princes of Judah have become like those who move a boundary marker; upon them I will pour out my wrath like water.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Moving a boundary marker (gevul, H1366) was a grave offense in ancient Israel, condemned explicitly in Deuteronomy 19:14 and 27:17, because boundary stones defined the covenantal inheritance of each tribe. THEOLOGICAL: Judah's princes are condemned alongside Ephraim in this chapter, indicating that the covenant failure is not merely northern — the twin judgment on both kingdoms anticipates the Assyrian and Babylonian exiles as sequential acts of YHWH's covenant enforcement.

verses.flow: Ephraim is oppressed, crushed in judgment, because he was pleased to walk after the command (tzav).
And I am like a moth to Ephraim, and like rottenness to the house of Judah.

conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: When Ephraim saw his sickness, and Judah his wound, then Ephraim went to Assyria and sent to the great king (melek yarev); but he is not able to heal you, and he cannot cure your wound.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The great king" translates melek yarev (H3377), a title of uncertain meaning — possibly a personal name or throne title for an Assyrian monarch, perhaps Tiglath-Pileser III, to whom both Israel and Judah paid tribute during this period (2 Kings 15:19; 16:7-8). The futility of seeking Assyria's help is a repeated prophetic theme: the very empire they bribe becomes the instrument of their destruction. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH alone is the healer of Israel (Exodus 15:26; Hosea 6:1) — the turn to foreign political powers is a direct rejection of covenant dependence.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: For I am like a lion to Ephraim, and like a young lion to the house of Judah — I, even I, will tear and go away; I will carry off, and none shall rescue.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The lion imagery for YHWH (ari, H738; kephir, H3715) is striking — the God of the covenant becomes the covenant's enforcer of curses (Leviticus 26:22). DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the ANE, the lion was a royal and divine symbol; YHWH's self-presentation as lion overtly displaces the claims of pagan deities. The tearing and withdrawal imagery prefigures a sovereign retreat that leaves the people defenseless, anticipating the "hiding of the face" theology (hester panim) of Deuteronomy 31:17-18.

verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: I will go and return to my place, until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face; in their distress they will seek me earnestly (shachar).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's return to "his place" (maqom, H4725) implies a withdrawal of the divine presence — a profound covenantal sanction in which the Shekinah glory departs, as later dramatized in Ezekiel 10-11. The word shachar (H7836), translated "seek earnestly," carries the nuance of seeking at dawn with intense diligence, expressing desperate longing. TYPOLOGY: The conditional "until they acknowledge their guilt" points toward eschatological restoration; the NT reads this trajectory as fulfilled in the repentance and return of Israel through Messiah (Romans 11:25-26; Zechariah 12:10).

verses.flow: Come, and let us return to YHWH; for it is He who has torn, and He will heal us; He has struck, and He will bind us up.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The call to "return to YHWH" (Hebrew: shuv, H7725) establishes the penitential pattern of Israel's restoration. The imagery of YHWH who "tears and heals" (cf. Deut 32:39) is the language of covenant discipline followed by covenant renewal, anticipating the ultimate healing wrought through the Suffering Servant (Isa 53:5). THEOLOGICAL: The verbs "torn" and "struck" describe divine chastisement within the covenant relationship, not abandonment — YHWH wounds only to restore.

verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common plur

verses.flow: He will revive us after two days; on the third day He will raise us up, and we shall live before His face.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "On the third day He will raise us up" is among the most striking resurrection foreshadowings in the Hebrew prophets. Paul appears to anchor the universal early Christian confession of Christ's resurrection "on the third day according to the Scriptures" (1 Cor 15:4) partly in this text. HEBREW: The verb "yaqimenu" (H6965, qum) means to rise, stand up, or be raised — the same root used of resurrection hope in Job 19:26 and Isa 26:19. THEOLOGICAL: The corporate "us" suggests that Israel's national restoration is bound up typologically with bodily resurrection.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common plur
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And let us know — let us press on to know YHWH; His going forth is as certain as the dawn; and He will come to us as the rain, as the latter rain (yoreh) that waters the earth.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yoreh" (H3138) refers to the early autumn rain that prepares the ground for planting, as distinct from the "malkosh" or latter spring rain. Both rains were essential in ancient Canaan's agricultural cycle and became symbols of covenant blessing (Deut 11:14). TYPOLOGY: YHWH's coming "as the rain" anticipates the outpouring of the Spirit (Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2), with the regular seasonal rains serving as a natural sacrament of divine faithfulness.

verses.flow: What shall I do with you, O Ephraim? What shall I do with you, O Judah? For your covenant-loyalty (hesed) is like a morning cloud, and like the dew that goes away early.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hesed" (H2617) is the covenant word par excellence — denoting loyal, steadfast love that is obligated by relationship. Its comparison to "morning cloud" (anan boqer) that evaporates captures Israel's pattern of superficial, transient repentance. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's lament — framed as a question — reveals the pathos at the heart of the covenant; the divine grief over fickle loyalty is a window into the character of God as genuinely relational.

words.g: in/with a prophet or inspired man

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Therefore I have hewn them by the prophets; I have slain them by the words of My mouth; and your judgments go forth as the light.
For I desire covenant-loyalty (hesed), and not sacrifice; and the knowledge of God more than burnt offerings.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew "chaphets" (H2654, "I desire") is a strong volitional term meaning to delight in or take pleasure in. The contrast is not an abolition of sacrifice but a prioritization: external ritual without inner hesed is hollow. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus cites this verse twice (Matt 9:13; 12:7), applying it against the Pharisees' misuse of religious categories. It stands as one of the most important prophetic critiques of religion divorced from covenant character. TYPOLOGY: Christ Himself is the fulfillment of true hesed — His self-offering is the one sacrifice that is simultaneously perfect obedience and perfect love.

words.g: as/like Adam the name of the first man

verses.flow: But they, like Adam, have transgressed the covenant; there they have dealt treacherously against Me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "K'adam" (H120) is debated: it may mean "like Adam" (the first man), "like men" (generically), or refer to a place called Adam (cf. Josh 3:16). The "like Adam" reading is the most theologically charged, casting Israel's covenant transgression as a recapitulation of the original fall — both parties in a covenant of life choosing rebellion. THEOLOGICAL: The word "bagad" (treachery, H898) is the language of marital betrayal, linking back to Hosea's central marriage metaphor.

words.g: from blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: Gilead is a city of workers of iniquity, tracked with blood.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb piel infinitive construct

verses.flow: And as bands of robbers lie in wait for a man, so the company of priests murder on the road to Shechem; for they commit wickedness of deliberate intent.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Shechem was a city of refuge (Josh 20:7) and a sacred assembly site (Josh 24). Priestly violence on the road to Shechem is therefore a profound desecration — those appointed as mediators of life become agents of death. THEOLOGICAL: The deliberate wickedness of the priests ("zimmah," H2154 — wicked planning) shows that this is not inadvertent sin but premeditated apostasy, heightening the covenant indictment.

verses.flow: In the house of Israel I have seen a horrible thing; there Ephraim's harlotry is found; Israel is defiled.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: Also, O Judah, a harvest is appointed for you, when I restore the fortunes of My people.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "harvest appointed" (Hebrew: qatsir, H7105) for Judah is deliberately ambiguous — it may signify judgment (as in Joel 3:13; Rev 14:15) or restoration (as in the following verse Hos 7:1 and the book's ultimate hope in Hos 14). TYPOLOGY: The phrase "when I restore the fortunes of My people" (Hebrew: b'shuvi sh'vut ammi) is a formulaic expression of eschatological restoration appearing also in Jer 30:3 and Amos 9:14, pointing toward the ultimate ingathering of Israel under the Davidic Messiah.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix
conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: When I would heal Israel, the iniquity of Ephraim is uncovered, and the evils of Samaria; for they deal falsely, and the thief enters in, and the band of robbers raids outside.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "heal" (rapha, H7495) sets up a bitter irony — YHWH's desire to restore Israel is immediately met with the exposure of deeper sin. "Ephraim" (H669) is used here as the leading tribe of the northern kingdom, a synecdoche for all Israel. "Samaria" names the capital city, indicating institutional corruption reaching the highest levels of government. The term "falsehood" or dealing falsely (shaqer, H8267) implies a systematic, habitual practice of deception.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: And they do not say to their hearts that I remember all their evil. Now their deeds surround them; they are before my face.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "they do not say to their hearts" reflects the Hebrew idiom for internal moral reasoning — the heart (lev, H3820) is the seat of mind and will in Hebrew anthropology, not merely emotion. YHWH's declaration that "I remember all their evil" stands in stark covenantal contrast: Israel has forgotten YHWH (cf. Hos 4:6), but YHWH forgets nothing. Their deeds "surround them" — a forensic image of evidence encircling the accused.

words.g: and + in/with literally a failure of flesh

verses.flow: By their evil they make the king glad, and the princes by their treacheries.
They are all adulterers; like a heated oven — the baker ceases to stir from the kneading of the dough until it is leavened.
verses.note: HEBREW: The oven (tannur, H8574) metaphor dominates verses 4–7, depicting Israel's burning, uncontrolled passions — particularly political conspiracy and sexual immorality. The baker's ceasing to stir suggests a consuming fire that feeds itself without intervention, picturing sin's self-perpetuating momentum. "Adulterers" (mena'aphim, H5003) carries both literal and covenantal-theological weight — Israel has committed spiritual adultery against YHWH (cf. Hos 1–3).

verses.flow: On the day of our king, the princes made him sick with the heat of wine; he stretched out his hand with mockers.
For they draw near with their heart like an oven in their ambush; their baker sleeps all the night; in the morning it burns like a flaming fire.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: They are all hot as an oven, and they devour their judges; all their kings have fallen — none among them calls to me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The indictment "none among them calls to me" (YHWH) is the theological climax of the oven sequence (vv. 4–7). Political chaos — kings assassinated, judges consumed — is traced not to geopolitical failure but to covenantal abandonment. This verse anticipates the northern kingdom's rapid succession of assassinated kings in the final decades before 722 BC (cf. 2 Kgs 15). TYPOLOGY: The failure of earthly kings here underscores the necessity of the coming messianic king who never forsakes the Father (Ps 2; John 8:29).

verses.flow: Ephraim — he mixes himself among the peoples; Ephraim is a cake not turned.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A cake not turned" (ugah lo haphukah, H5692) is a striking domestic image — a flatbread burned on one side and raw on the other, entirely ruined and useless. This depicts Ephraim's syncretism: half-assimilated into pagan nations, half-Israelite, wholly compromised. "Mixes himself" (yitbolal, from balal, H1101) echoes the confusion of Babel — the same root — suggesting Ephraim's self-mixture is a kind of anti-creation disorder.

verses.flow: Strangers have devoured his strength, yet he does not know it; also gray hairs are sprinkled upon him, yet he does not know it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Strangers have devoured his strength" refers historically to the tribute payments and political subjugation Ephraim paid to Assyria (cf. 2 Kgs 15:19–20; 17:3). "Gray hairs sprinkled upon him" is a poignant image of a nation in terminal decline, aged without wisdom, oblivious to its own decay. The double refrain "yet he does not know it" stresses covenantal blindness — the prophetic diagnosis of a people whose spiritual senses have been seared (cf. Isa 6:9–10).

verb piel perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: And the pride of Israel testifies against him to his face, yet they do not return to YHWH their God, nor do they seek him in all this.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And Ephraim has become like a dove, silly and without sense — they call to Egypt, they go to Assyria.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The dove (yonah, H3123) — "silly and without sense" (Hebrew: peti, H6601, also the word for "simple" or "naive") — captures Ephraim's futile vacillation between Egypt and Assyria as political patrons, a policy documented in the final years of the northern kingdom (cf. 2 Kgs 17:4; Isa 30:1–5). THEOLOGICAL: True security comes only from YHWH, and seeking it from imperial powers is portrayed as foolish naivety bordering on culpable stupidity.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: As they go, I will spread my net over them; like the birds of the heavens I will bring them down; I will discipline them according to the report made to their assembly.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "according to the report made to their assembly" is textually difficult (MT: ke-shoma' la-adatam) but may allude to a decree issued in the divine council assembly (edah, H5712), i.e., YHWH's heavenly court has already rendered judgment on Ephraim's conduct, and the historical punishment will execute that verdict. This reading coheres with divine council theology found in Ps 82:1; 1 Kgs 22:19–23; and Job 1–2.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Woe to them, for they have strayed from me! Destruction to them, for they have transgressed against me! And I myself would redeem them, yet they speak lies against me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Hebrew woe-cry (hoy, H1945) signals a funeral lament — YHWH mourns over those he pronounces judgment upon, a mark of his hesed (H2617, covenant-loyalty) even in wrath. "I myself would redeem them" (anoki epadhem) — the first-person pronoun is emphatic in Hebrew, underscoring divine initiative in redemption — "yet they speak lies against me" shows the perversity of rejecting so gracious an offer. TYPOLOGY: This tension between divine desire to redeem and Israel's rejection foreshadows the Passion narrative (Matt 23:37; Luke 13:34).

verses.flow: And they do not cry to me from their heart, but they wail upon their beds; for grain and new wine they gash themselves; they turn away from me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "They gash themselves" (yitgoreriu, from gur or gore', cf. LXX and various MT readings) likely refers to mourning rites or cultic self-laceration associated with Baal worship (cf. 1 Kgs 18:28), practiced in the context of seeking grain and wine — agricultural fertility petitioned from the wrong deity. The phrase "they do not cry to me from their heart" (lo za'aqu elai bilibam) reiterates the heart-problem of verse 2: the cult is active but the covenant relationship is dead.

verses.flow: Though I trained and strengthened their arms, yet against me they devise evil.

words.g: from strictly froth at the mouth

verses.flow: They return, but not to the Most High (El-Elyon); they are like a deceitful bow; their princes shall fall by the sword because of the insolence of their tongue — this shall be their derision in the land of Egypt.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "They return, but not to the Most High" — the Hebrew El-Elyon (H5945) is the ancient name for the supreme God of the divine council, used here to emphasize that Ephraim's repentance is directed toward other divine beings or powers rather than YHWH himself. "A deceitful bow" (qeshet remiyyah, H7423) is an arrow that misses its mark despite being drawn — effort expended in entirely the wrong direction. HISTORICAL: "The land of Egypt" as the place of their derision closes a bitter circle — the nation redeemed from Egypt now returns to Egypt's scorn, reversing the Exodus (cf. Hos 8:13; 9:3).

verses.flow: Set the shofar (shofar) to your mouth! Like an eagle over the house of YHWH — because they have transgressed My covenant and against My law they have rebelled.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shofar" (H7782, shofar) is a ram's horn trumpet used for war alarms and sacred assembly. The command here is military-urgent. "Covenant" (H1285, berit) is the foundational Sinai covenant relationship; its violation is the central charge of the chapter. The image of the eagle (or vulture, H5404, nesher) signals a predatory invading force, likely Assyria, swooping upon a defenseless nation.

verses.flow: To Me they will cry out: "My God, we know You, we Israel!"

verses.flow: Israel has rejected the good; the enemy shall pursue him.
They have made kings, but not through Me; they have set up princes, but I did not know it. Their silver and their gold they have made into idols for themselves, to the end that it be cut off.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The setting up of kings "not through Me" indicts the northern kingdom's history from Jeroboam onward, where dynastic succession was achieved through violence and conspiracy rather than divine designation. This reflects the covenant stipulation that YHWH alone is Israel's ultimate King. The making of idols from silver and gold echoes the golden calves of Jeroboam (1 Kgs 12:28), a persistent sin that culminated in national destruction.

verses.flow: He has cast off your calf, O Samaria; My anger is kindled against them. How long will they be incapable of innocence?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The divine speech shifts pointedly to address Samaria's calf idol, the central religious symbol of the northern kingdom's counterfeit worship. The phrase "My anger is kindled" (H639, aph) is the burning of the divine nostrils, a vivid anthropomorphism expressing intense holy wrath. The rhetorical question "How long will they be incapable of innocence?" underscores the deep moral incapacity produced by sustained idolatry.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For it is from Israel — even this! A craftsman made it; it is not God. For the calf of Samaria shall be broken in pieces.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: For they sow the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind. Standing grain there is none for it; the sprout shall yield no meal; if it should yield, strangers would swallow it up.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The agricultural metaphor "sow the wind, reap the whirlwind" (H5492, suphah) is one of the most memorable statements of consequence theology in the prophets. The wind (H7307, ruach) here is not the life-giving breath of God but empty, destructive nothingness. The principle of compounded consequence — sowing emptiness yields catastrophic destruction — is cited as a general wisdom maxim throughout later tradition.

verses.flow: Israel is swallowed up; now they are among the nations like a vessel in which there is no delight.
For they have gone up to Assyria, a wild donkey wandering alone; Ephraim has hired lovers for himself.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "wild donkey wandering alone" (H6501, pere) is a powerful image of willful isolation and untameable stubbornness. Ephraim's diplomatic overtures to Assyria (cf. 2 Kgs 15:19; 17:3) are cast as spiritual harlotry — "hiring lovers" — a deliberate echo of the marriage metaphor established in chapters 1–3. Seeking Assyrian military alliance rather than YHWH was the definitive act of covenant adultery.

conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: Though they hire among the nations, now I will gather them, and they shall begin to diminish under the burden of the king of princes.
Because Ephraim has multiplied altars for sinning, altars have become to him for sinning.
I wrote for him the many things of My law (torah), but they were regarded as a strange thing.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451, torah) derives from the root yarah, meaning to throw or to direct, hence instruction or teaching. The phrase "the many things of My law" stresses the abundance and comprehensiveness of divine revelation already given. The tragic irony is that what YHWH wrote abundantly was treated as something "foreign" (H2114, zur) — alien, strange, belonging to another. This anticipates Paul's lament in Romans 10:21.

verses.flow: As for the sacrifices of My offerings, they sacrifice meat and eat it, but YHWH does not accept them. Now He will remember their iniquity and punish their sins; they shall return to Egypt.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The threat "they shall return to Egypt" is a devastating reversal of the Exodus, the defining salvific event in Israel's memory. To return to Egypt is to undo redemption itself. This typological inversion — from liberation to re-enslavement — foreshadows the NT pattern where rejection of the Redeemer results in bondage. The principle that YHWH does not accept sacrifices divorced from covenantal fidelity is cited by Jesus in Matt 9:13, quoting Hos 6:6.

verses.flow: For Israel has forgotten his Maker and has built palaces, and Judah has multiplied fortified cities; so I will send a fire upon his cities, and it shall devour her strongholds.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with the indictment that Israel has "forgotten his Maker" (H6213, asah — the one who made/formed him), trading trust in YHWH for trust in fortified architecture. Judah is included in the judgment here, indicating that the prophet's indictment is not merely sectarian but applies to the whole covenant people. Fire upon cities echoes the destruction of Sodom and signals the withdrawal of divine protection from a people who have abandoned their divine patron.

verses.flow: Rejoice not, O Israel! Exult not like the peoples! For you have played the harlot, departing from your God; you have loved the harlot's fee upon all threshing floors of grain.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "zanah" (to play the harlot, H2181) is a central metaphor in Hosea, linking Israel's idolatry directly to the prophet's own marital experience (Hos 1-3). The phrase "etnan zonah" (harlot's fee) echoes Deut 23:18, where such payment is forbidden as an offering to YHWH. The "threshing floors" were sites of both agricultural celebration and Canaanite fertility-cult worship, making the setting deeply ironic.

verses.flow: Threshing floor and winepress shall not feed them, and the new wine shall fail her.

verses.flow: They shall not dwell in the land of YHWH, but Ephraim shall return to Egypt, and they shall eat unclean food in Assyria.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Return to Egypt represents a covenantal curse — the reversal of the Exodus (Deut 28:68). The phrase "eretz YHWH" (land of YHWH) is theologically significant: Canaan is not merely a geographic inheritance but YHWH's own dwelling domain. Exile to Assyria means eating "unclean" (tameh) food — a status-loss of covenantal holiness, not merely ritual impurity. TYPOLOGY: The Exodus pattern of departure and return echoes in Matt 2:15, where Christ recapitulates Israel's calling "out of Egypt."

verses.flow: They shall not pour out wine offerings to YHWH, and their sacrifices shall not please him; like the bread of mourners it shall be to them — all who eat of it shall be defiled; for their bread shall be for their appetite only; it shall not come into the house of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bread of mourners" (lechem onim) refers to food rendered ritually impure through contact with the dead (Num 19:14). The sacrifices here are described as not "pleasing" (arob, H6148 / ratsah) to YHWH — the technical term for divine acceptance of sacrifice — signaling the total rupture of the sacrificial system between Israel and God. THEOLOGICAL: Worship without covenant faithfulness becomes self-serving consumption rather than communion.

verses.flow: What will you do on the day of appointed feast, and on the day of the feast of YHWH?

verses.flow: For behold, they have gone away from devastation; Egypt shall gather them, Memphis shall bury them. Their precious things of silver — nettles shall possess them; thorns shall be in their tents.

verses.flow: The days of punishment have come; the days of recompense have come. Israel shall know it. The prophet is a fool, the man of the spirit is mad — because of the greatness of your iniquity and great hatred.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pakudah" (punishment/visitation, H6486) and "shillum" (recompense, H7966) are legal-covenantal terms announcing divine judgment. The taunt "the prophet is a fool (ewil), the man of the spirit (ish ha-ruach) is mad" may reflect the people's mockery of true prophets like Hosea — or possibly Hosea's own ironic indictment of false prophets. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "ish ha-ruach" is notable as a designation for a prophet, connecting prophetic inspiration to the ruach (spirit) of YHWH.

verses.flow: The watchman of Ephraim is with my God; the prophet — a fowler's snare is on all his ways, hatred in the house of his God.
They have gone deep in their corruption, as in the days of Gibeah; he will remember their iniquity, he will punish their sins.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "days of Gibeah" refers to the catastrophic moral atrocity of Judges 19-20, when the men of Gibeah committed sexual violence resulting in a near-extinction of the tribe of Benjamin. This is Hosea's benchmark for Israel's deepest corruption. The phrase "he' ammiku" (they have gone deep) suggests deliberate, entrenched sinfulness rather than momentary failure.

verses.flow: Like grapes in the wilderness I found Israel; like the first fruit on the fig tree in its first season I saw your fathers. But they came to Baal-Peor and consecrated themselves to Shame, and they became detestable like the thing they loved.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Baal-Peor refers to the incident in Num 25:1-9 when Israel yoked itself to the Baal of Peor through sexual immorality and idolatrous worship, resulting in a plague that killed 24,000. "Consecrated themselves to Shame" (bosheth, H1322) is a scribal substitution for the divine name Baal, a common Masoretic polemical device. THEOLOGICAL: The transformation into the image of what one worships — "they became detestable like the thing they loved" — is a profound theological statement on the formative power of worship (cf. Ps 115:8; Rom 1:23).

verses.flow: Ephraim — their glory shall fly away like a bird: no birth, no pregnancy, no conception.

verses.flow: For even if they bring up children, I will bereave them until none is left. Yea, woe to them when I depart from them!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "be-shuvi me-hem" (when I depart from them) is among the most terrifying expressions of judgment in the prophets — not active wrath but divine withdrawal. The absence of YHWH is itself the ultimate curse (cf. Ps 22:1; Matt 27:46). This reflects the inverse of the Immanuel promise.

verses.flow: Ephraim, as I have seen, was like Tyre planted in a pleasant place; but Ephraim shall bring out his children to the slayer.
Give them, O YHWH — what will you give? Give them a miscarrying womb and dry breasts.

verses.flow: Every evil of theirs was at Gilgal; truly there I hated them. Because of the wickedness of their deeds I will drive them out of my house; I will love them no more; all their princes are rebels.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Gilgal was a sacred site where Israel first camped after crossing the Jordan (Josh 4-5) and where Saul was confirmed as king (1 Sam 11:15), but it became a center of apostate worship (Amos 4:4; 5:5). YHWH's declaration "I will drive them out of my house" (mibeiti) frames the covenant relationship in household terms — Israel as a rebellious member expelled from the divine household, an image developed further in the NT (Heb 3:6; 1 Pet 2:5).

verses.flow: My God will reject them, because they have not listened to him; and they shall be wanderers among the nations.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb "ma'as" (to reject, H3988) is a covenant-rupture term — YHWH's rejection of the people mirrors their own rejection of him through persistent disobedience (1 Sam 15:23; cf. Rom 11:1-2 where Paul insists God has not ultimately rejected his people). "Wanderers among the nations" (nodedim ba-goyim) echoes the curse of Cain (Gen 4:12) and prefigures the Diaspora. TYPOLOGY: The remnant theology implicit here — that rejection is not final — is the basis for Paul's argument in Romans 9-11 and the ingathering promised in Hos 14.

verses.flow: Israel is a luxuriant (bujah) vine; he brings forth fruit for himself. According to the abundance of his fruit he has multiplied altars; according to the goodness of his land they have made goodly pillars (matstsevot).
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening term rendered "luxuriant vine" comes from gefen boqeq (Strong's H1238), meaning a vine that empties or spreads itself out. The irony is sharp: Israel's material abundance was the very occasion for multiplying idolatrous altars and pillars (matstsevot, H4676), stone standing-monuments associated with Baalistic fertility worship throughout Canaan. THEOLOGICAL: Prosperity became the engine of apostasy — the more YHWH blessed, the more Israel attributed that blessing to false gods, a pattern condemned throughout the Deuteronomic tradition (Deut 32:15).

verses.flow: Their heart is divided (halaq); now they shall bear their guilt. He himself shall break down their altars; he shall destroy their pillars.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Divided" renders halaq (H2505), meaning smooth or slippery, here used for a heart that is split in allegiance — outwardly performing covenant ritual while inwardly devoted to Baal. THEOLOGICAL: The divided heart is the antithesis of the shema's call to love YHWH with the whole heart (Deut 6:4-5). The consequence is juridical: they "shall bear their guilt" (asham, H816), a term with both relational and legal weight in covenant theology.

verses.flow: For now they shall say, "We have no king, for we do not fear YHWH; and as for the king, what can he do for us?"

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: They speak words, swearing falsely in making covenants; so judgment (mishpat) springs up like poisonous weeds in the furrows of the field.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Poisonous weeds" translates rosh (H7219), a bitter and toxic plant often rendered "hemlock" or "gall" (cf. Amos 6:12). The image of mishpat (justice/judgment, H4941) springing up as poison in plowed furrows inverts the agricultural blessing of the covenant — the land that should yield life yields death because the covenant has been broken through false oaths. THEOLOGICAL: Covenant oaths sworn falsely (alah shav, vain swearing) carry inherent self-cursing; the land itself becomes an instrument of judgment.

verses.flow: The inhabitants of Samaria tremble for the calf of Beth-aven. For its people mourn over it, and its priests who rejoiced over it — over its glory — for it has departed from it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Beth-aven" (House of Wickedness/Nothingness) is a polemical name for Bethel (House of God), where Jeroboam I erected a golden calf (1 Kgs 12:28-29). The priests here (kemarim, H3649) are a specific word for idolatrous priests, used with contempt, distinguished from Levitical kohanim. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The departure of the idol's "glory" (kabod, H3519) may echo the theology of divine abandonment — YHWH's glory departing from Israel as it departed from the tabernacle (Ezek 10:18), leaving only the hollow object.

verses.flow: Even it shall be carried to Assyria as tribute to the great king. Ephraim shall receive shame, and Israel shall be ashamed of his own counsel.
Samaria's king shall perish like a chip on the face of the waters.

verses.flow: The high places (bamot) of Aven, the sin of Israel, shall be destroyed. Thorn and thistle shall grow up on their altars, and they shall say to the mountains, "Cover us," and to the hills, "Fall on us."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The cry "Cover us" and "Fall on us" to mountains and hills is quoted verbatim in Luke 23:30 by Jesus as he speaks to the daughters of Jerusalem on the way to the cross, and echoed in Revelation 6:16 at the sixth seal. The language of cosmic dread and the desire for annihilation before divine wrath forms a profound eschatological bridge: what Israel faces in the Assyrian judgment foreshadows the final day of YHWH's wrath. HEBREW: "High places" (bamot, H1116) were elevated worship sites, often pre-Israelite Canaanite sanctuaries appropriated for syncretistic use.

verses.flow: "From the days of Gibeah you have sinned, O Israel; there they have stood. Shall not the battle against the children of iniquity overtake them in Gibeah?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to Gibeah invokes one of the darkest episodes in Israel's history — the gang rape and murder of the Levite's concubine and the ensuing civil war (Judg 19-21). For Hosea, Gibeah functions as the paradigmatic moment of Israel's moral collapse, the original wound from which the nation never recovered. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "there they have stood" implies a persistent, unrepentant posture — Gibeah's sin is not past history but present identity.

verses.flow: When I desire, I will chastise them; and nations shall be gathered against them when they are bound in their double iniquity.
And Ephraim is a trained heifer that loves to thresh; so I passed over her fair neck. I will put Ephraim to the yoke; Judah shall plow; Jacob shall break his clods.
verses.note: HEBREW: The agricultural imagery shifts from free threshing to yoked plowing — an escalation of labor and constraint. A heifer that threshes (doshah, H1758) walks freely over the grain and may eat as she works (Deut 25:4), but a yoked animal is under total direction. Ephraim's judgment is this loss of freedom. TYPOLOGY: Paul cites the threshing-ox law (Deut 25:4) in 1 Cor 9:9 and 1 Tim 5:18 to argue for workers deserving their wages — the agricultural metaphor carries into NT ethics.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Sow for yourselves righteousness (tsedaqah); reap according to steadfast-love (hesed); break up your fallow ground, for it is time to seek YHWH, until he comes and rains righteousness upon you.
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse forms the theological heart of the chapter and perhaps of the entire book's call to repentance. "Sow" and "reap" are covenant antitheses to the perverse harvest of v.13. Hesed (H2617) — steadfast-love, covenant-loyalty, lovingkindness — is the fruit of righteous sowing. "Break up your fallow ground" (nir lachem nir, H5215) is a call to interior preparation, not merely external ritual. THEOLOGICAL: "Until he comes and rains righteousness upon you" carries messianic weight — the longing for YHWH's sovereign coming that culminates in Christ's first and second advents (cf. Jas 5:7). TYPOLOGY: Jesus' words in Matt 9:13 citing Hosea 6:6 ("I desire hesed, not sacrifice") are the hermeneutical key for reading all of Hosea's agricultural/covenant imagery.

verses.flow: You have plowed wickedness; you have reaped injustice; you have eaten the fruit of lies. Because you have trusted in your own way, in the multitude of your mighty men,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chain of moral causality is precise and devastating: you plowed wickedness, you reaped injustice, you ate the fruit of lies. Each stage compounds the previous. "Trusted in your own way" (derek, H1870) identifies self-reliance and military confidence as the spiritual root of the apostasy — the nation looked to chariots and warriors rather than to YHWH (cf. Ps 20:7; Isa 31:1). This is covenant unfaithfulness expressed as geopolitical idolatry.

verses.flow: therefore the tumult of war shall arise among your people, and all your fortresses shall be destroyed, as Shalman destroyed Beth-arbel on the day of battle; mothers were dashed in pieces upon their children.
Thus it shall be done to you, O Bethel, because of your great evil. At dawn the king of Israel shall be utterly cut off."
verses.note: HEBREW: "At dawn" (be-shachar, H7837) suggests the sudden and early nature of the judgment — the king is cut off at the very moment the day begins, without warning or reprieve. The verb rendered "utterly cut off" is a Niphal infinitive absolute construction (dom yiddom, H1820), emphasizing total and final silence/destruction. THEOLOGICAL: The final silencing of the Israelite monarchy stands as a covenantal warning: the kings of the northern kingdom, inaugurated in rebellion (1 Kgs 12), end in silence. Only the Davidic line through Judah carries the messianic promise forward to its fulfillment in Christ (Luke 1:32-33).

verses.flow: When Israel was a youth, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called my son.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase "out of Egypt I called my son" (mimitzrayim qarati livni) refers historically to the Exodus of Israel as YHWH's corporate son (Exod 4:22), yet Matthew 2:15 applies it directly to Jesus' return from Egypt, employing a typological fulfillment in which the Son of God recapitulates and perfects Israel's history. This is not Matthew misreading the text but recognizing that the Messiah embodies and completes Israel's calling. HEBREW: The word "youth" (na'ar, H5288) suggests tender early age, emphasizing the grace and initiative of YHWH's love preceding any merit on Israel's part.

verses.flow: The more they were called, the more they went from them; to the Baals they sacrificed, and to the carved images they made offerings.

verses.flow: Yet it was I who taught Ephraim to walk; I took them up by their arms, but they did not know that I healed them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of YHWH teaching Ephraim to walk as a parent teaches a child to take its first steps is among the most tender parental metaphors in the prophetic corpus. The verb "healed" (rapha, H7495) recalls YHWH's self-revelation as YHWH Rapha (Exod 15:26), the one who heals his people. Israel's failure to acknowledge divine provision mirrors the broader covenant indictment: they attributed their blessings to the Baals (Hos 2:8).

verses.flow: With cords of a man I led them, with bands of love (hesed); and I was to them as those who lift the yoke from upon their jaws, and I bent down to them and fed them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cords of a man" (avot adam) contrasts with cords of an animal, implying that YHWH led Israel with bonds befitting human dignity rather than brute compulsion. "Bands of love" may also be rendered "ropes of hesed (lovingkindness, H2617)," the covenant loyalty term central to Hosea's theology. The image of lifting the yoke from the jaws is disputed textually; some emend to "I was like one who eases the yoke on their cheeks," depicting tender relief from oppressive labor.

verses.flow: How can I give you up, O Ephraim? How can I hand you over, O Israel? How can I make you like Admah? How can I treat you like Zeboiim? My heart is turned within me; my compassions (nihumim) are kindled together.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 8 records a dramatic divine soliloquy in which YHWH wrestles with the tension between justice and covenant love (hesed). Admah and Zeboiim were cities destroyed with Sodom and Gomorrah (Deut 29:23), evoking total annihilation. The phrase "my heart is turned within me" (nehpak libbi) uses the same root as the overthrow (hapak) of Sodom, suggesting YHWH's heart is "overthrown" — undone by compassion — in place of Israel being overthrown. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term "nihumim" (compassions, H5150, from nacham) describes the visceral stirring of divine pathos, a concept Heschel identified as central to prophetic theology — God is not impassive but genuinely moved by the plight of his covenant people.

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: I will not execute the burning of my anger; I will not again destroy Ephraim; for I am God and not a man, the Holy One in your midst, and I will not come in wrath.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The declaration "I am God and not a man" (El anochi velo ish) grounds YHWH's restraint of wrath not in weakness but in the ontological difference between divine and human nature. Where a human king might exact total retribution, YHWH's holiness paradoxically expresses itself in mercy. "The Holy One in your midst" (qadosh beqirbekha) is a remarkable statement: holiness does not drive YHWH away from a sinful people but positions him redemptively among them. (Isa 12:6; Rev 21:3)

verses.flow: They shall walk after YHWH; he shall roar like a lion, and when he roars, then sons shall come trembling from the west.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's roaring "like a lion" reverses the lion imagery of judgment used earlier in Hosea (5:14; 13:7-8), transforming the same image into one of gathering and restoration. Sons trembling "from the west" (miyyam, literally "from the sea") may indicate the Mediterranean diaspora. The lion's roar as a signal of divine assembly and return echoes Amos 3:8 and anticipates the eschatological ingathering. TYPOLOGY: The gathering of dispersed Israel foreshadows the new exodus accomplished through Messiah, in whom the nations and scattered Israel are united (Eph 1:10; John 11:52).

verses.flow: Ephraim feeds on wind and pursues the east wind all the day; he multiplies lies and violence (chamas); and they make a covenant with Assyria, and oil is carried to Egypt.
verses.note: HEBREW: "chamas" (Strong's H2555) denotes violence, wrongdoing, or injustice — frequently used in prophetic indictments. "East wind" (ruach qadim) evokes the scorching sirocco, a symbol of destructive futility. The verse sets up a double indictment: false alliances with world powers (Assyria and Egypt) as the practical expression of spiritual infidelity. THEOLOGICAL: Feeding on wind is a metaphor for the vanity of trusting human political powers over YHWH — anticipating NT warnings against worldly alliance over covenant faithfulness (Matt 6:24).

verses.flow: YHWH has a controversy (riv) with Judah and will punish Jacob according to his ways; according to his deeds he will repay him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Riv" (Strong's H7379) is a covenant lawsuit term — YHWH as the divine suzerain bringing formal charges against his vassal. The shift from Ephraim (v.1) to Judah here is notable textually; some interpreters read "Israel" with LXX. The paired terms "ways" and "deeds" stress moral accountability grounded in covenantal conduct.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: In the womb he seized his brother by the heel, and in his manhood he strove with God (Elohim).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The Jacob narrative (Gen 25:26; 32:28) is invoked typologically — Jacob the supplanter becomes the pattern for Israel's wrestling with God. The heel-grasping (aqev, Strong's H6117) is the etymological root of "Jacob" (Ya'aqov). Hosea uses this history not as praise but as a mirror of Israel's persistent striving against its covenant Lord.

verses.flow: He strove with the angel (malak) and prevailed; he wept and sought his favor. At Bethel he found him, and there he spoke with us —
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Malak" (Strong's H4397) — angel or divine messenger. The Bethel encounter conflates Gen 32 (Peniel) and Gen 28 (Bethel), suggesting Hosea is theologically compressing these events. Jacob's weeping and seeking favor (wayitchanan) echoes priestly language of petition. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "he spoke with us" is startling — it draws the present generation into the patriarchal encounter, asserting covenantal solidarity across generations.

verses.flow: YHWH, the God (Elohim) of hosts, YHWH is his memorial name (zeker).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Zeker" (Strong's H2143) — memorial name or remembrance-name. This is related to "zakar," to remember. The title "YHWH of hosts" (YHWH Tseva'ot) is a divine council title, denoting YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The designation "God of hosts" carries the full weight of YHWH's sovereignty over all divine beings and cosmic powers — his name is itself a proclamation of lordship (Ps 46:7; Isa 6:3).

verses.flow: So you, return to your God (Elohim); keep steadfast love (hesed) and justice (mishpat), and wait continually for your God (Elohim).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hesed" (Strong's H2617) — covenant love, lovingkindness, steadfast loyalty. "Mishpat" (Strong's H4941) — justice, judgment, righteous order. "Qavah" (wait, Strong's H6960) carries the sense of expectant, hope-filled waiting. TYPOLOGY: The call to return (shuv) to God through hesed and mishpat echoes Micah 6:8 and anticipates the great commandment synthesis of love and justice in Christ (Matt 22:37-40).

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: A merchant (kena'an) — in his hands are scales of deceit; he loves to oppress.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kena'an" (Strong's H3667) — literally "Canaan," but used here as a wordplay for "merchant" or "trader" (a common ANE semantic range). Hosea uses this as a biting epithet: Ephraim has become indistinguishable from the Canaanites they were supposed to displace. The scales of deceit (mozney mirmah) recall Amos 8:5. HISTORICAL: Dishonest weights were condemned throughout the ancient Near East, attested in both Mesopotamian law codes and Egyptian wisdom literature.

verses.flow: And Ephraim has said, "Surely I have become rich, I have found wealth for myself; in all my labors they will find in me no iniquity that is sin."

verses.flow: But I am YHWH your God from the land of Egypt; I will again make you dwell in tents, as in the days of the appointed feast (mo'ed).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH reasserts his identity through the Exodus — the foundational redemptive event. The promise/threat to make Israel dwell in tents again evokes wilderness discipline. "Mo'ed" (Strong's H4150) — appointed feast or set time, specifically evoking the Feast of Booths (Sukkot), when Israel commemorated wilderness dwelling (Lev 23:42-43). TYPOLOGY: Dwelling in tents points forward to the eschatological "tabernacling" of God with his people (John 1:14; Rev 21:3).

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I spoke to the prophets; it was I who multiplied visions, and through the prophets I gave parables (damah).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's multiplication of visions and parables through the prophets is itself a merciful act — repeated warning before judgment. "Damah" (Strong's H1819) — to liken, compare, use similitude or parable. The prophetic office is here presented as the continuation of divine speech, emphasizing that Israel's failure is inexcusable given the abundance of revelation (Heb 1:1-2).

verses.flow: And by a prophet YHWH brought Israel up from Egypt, and by a prophet he was kept (guarded).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Moses the prophet is here unnamed but clearly intended — Israel's entire national existence owes to prophetic mediation. The double use of "guarded/kept" (shamar, Strong's H8104) frames Moses' prophetic role as a shepherd-guardian. TYPOLOGY: Moses as mediator-prophet prefigures Christ as the ultimate Prophet (Deut 18:15; Acts 3:22), who leads the new exodus of redemption out of spiritual bondage.

verses.flow: Ephraim has bitterly provoked him to anger; so his Lord (Adonav) will leave upon him his bloodguilt, and will turn back upon him his reproach.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Adonav" — his Lord, with the possessive suffix on Adon (Strong's H113). This is a weighty choice: YHWH is not merely Israel's God but its sovereign Master, whose patience has limits. "Bloodguilt" (damim, Strong's H1818) — the guilt of shed blood; "reproach" (cherpah, Strong's H2781) — taunt, disgrace. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends with judgment language that makes Ephraim's ruin its own self-authored sentence — their sins return upon their own head, a pattern of covenantal retribution (Deut 28).

verses.flow: When Ephraim spoke, there was trembling; he was exalted in Israel. But he incurred guilt through Baal and died.
verses.note: HEBREW: "There was trembling" (charedah, H2731) denotes a reverential fear or shaking that accompanied Ephraim's political and cultic dominance in the northern kingdom. The verb "died" (wayamot) is pointed as a simple past, indicating the spiritual death that came through Baal worship as a completed historical reality rather than merely a future threat. The text recalls the Baal-Peor episode (Num 25) as the paradigmatic moment of apostasy.

verses.flow: And now they sin more and more, and they have made for themselves molten images from their silver, idols according to their own understanding — all of them the work of craftsmen. To these they say, "Those who sacrifice men kiss calves!"
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase often rendered "those who sacrifice men kiss calves" (zovchei adam agalim yishaqun) is deliberately shocking — human sacrifice and calf veneration are equated as twin abominations. The "molten images" (massekot, H4541) echo the golden calf (Exod 32), drawing a direct line between Sinai apostasy and Ephraim's present idolatry. "According to their own understanding" (kitevunah lahem) underscores that idolatry is the supreme act of human self-sufficiency.

verses.flow: But I am YHWH your God from the land of Egypt; and a God besides me you shall not know, and there is no savior (moshia) besides me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The self-identification formula "I am YHWH your God from the land of Egypt" directly echoes the Decalogue prologue (Exod 20:2), grounding the covenant lawsuit in the foundational redemptive act of the Exodus. The word "savior" (moshia, H3467) carries the same root as the name Yeshua/Jesus, making this passage a powerful lexical anticipation of the one true Savior. No political alliance, no Baal, no king could fulfill what only YHWH provides.

verses.flow: According to their pasture they were satisfied; they were satisfied, and their heart was lifted up. Therefore they forgot me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse captures the perennial covenantal danger: prosperity leading to pride and forgetfulness of God. The repetition of "satisfied" (yisbeu... yisbeu) is emphatic in the Hebrew — abundance intensified the apostasy rather than gratitude. The pattern anticipates Jesus's parable of the prodigal son and Paul's warning in Deut 8:10-14 context.
